title: Stress–Strain Curve
subtitle: Stress-strain curve for Class 11 Physics (NCERT) — elastic limit, yield point, breaking stress

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
The stress–strain curve tells the complete story of how a material behaves from no load to breaking point.
Key points on the curve for a ductile metal like steel:
Proportional limit (P): Stress ∝ strain; Hooke's Law holds
Elastic limit (E): Material returns to original shape if unloaded (even if no longer linear)
Yield point (Y): Material starts to deform permanently — large strain for little extra stress
Ultimate tensile strength (U): Maximum stress the material can bear
Breaking/fracture point (B): Material fractures
Brittle materials (glass, ceramics) snap at the elastic limit with no plastic region. Ductile materials (steel, copper) have a long plastic region — they can be drawn into wires.

blocks.heading: The Formula
blocks.body:
From the stress–strain graph:
Slope in linear region = Young's Modulus Y
Area under curve = energy stored per unit volume (toughness)
Elastic region energy = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2Y}
Yield stress and Ultimate tensile strength (UTS) are read directly from the graph.
Elongation at fracture (ductility measure): \varepsilon_f = \Delta L_f / L

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: Mild steel vs. cast iron: Mild steel has a long plastic region — it bends visibly before breaking, giving warning before structural failure. Cast iron is brittle — it fractures with almost no plastic deformation. This is why building codes specify ductile materials for structural elements: a building that bends before it falls gives time to evacuate.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Slowly stretch a piece of sticky tape or a rubber band (rubber's curve is non-linear but instructive). Notice how it stretches easily at first, then resists more, then eventually tears. This maps to the stress–strain curve — you can feel the proportional, elastic, and fracture regions.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: Two materials have the same UTS but one has a long plastic region (ductile) and the other has almost none (brittle). The area under the stress–strain curve is the toughness — resistance to fracture. Which material absorbs more energy before breaking, and which would you prefer for a car bumper?

quiz.question: The area under the complete stress–strain curve represents:
options: Toughness — energy absorbed per unit volume before fracture
quiz.explanation: The area under the entire σ–ε curve (up to fracture) is the energy per unit volume the material can absorb before breaking — this is called toughness. The slope of the linear part is Young's modulus.
